policy_statement: Reduce waste sent to incinerator (white bags) by 30% by 2030.
mechanism_description: It aims to reduce the amount of residual waste sent to incineration, lowering emissions from waste combustion.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste management practice change → ↓ waste incinerated → ↓ combustion emissions (fossil CO₂ + other pollutants) → ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: The city reduces the share/quantity of residual municipal waste sent to incinerators by increasing prevention, reuse, recycling, separate collection, or alternative treatment pathways. (2) Mediating process: Less material is combusted, so less fossil carbon in the waste stream (e.g., plastics) is oxidized to CO₂ during incineration (and associated energy use at the facility can also decline). (3) Outcome: Lower direct, territorial GHG emissions from waste-to-energy/incineration, yielding net emissions reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions by avoiding waste combustion emissions (notably fossil CO₂) from incineration.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: divert less waste to incineration → reduce combustion of fossil-derived waste fractions → lower CO₂ emissions → mitigation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although diversion can improve material efficiency (resource efficiency) and sometimes affect local air quality, the stated and largest climate-relevant effect across this mechanism is reducing GHGs from incineration.  
3) Edge cases applied: If diverted waste is sent to landfill, methane could increase and erode/negate benefits; if diverted to recycling, additional upstream savings may occur—these are secondary/conditional effects and do not change that the mechanism’s defining pathway is incineration emissions avoidance.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy whose core mechanism is “reduce residual waste sent to incineration” shares the same primary mitigation pathway via avoided combustion emissions.  
5) Note: Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., prevention vs recycling vs alternative treatment, and net accounting depending on displaced energy) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net climate benefit can vary depending on where diverted waste goes (recycling/prevention vs landfill), the fossil/biogenic composition of the incinerated waste, and assumptions about energy recovery/displaced grid electricity; nevertheless the mechanism’s dominant intended effect is avoided incineration emissions.
key_indicators: “30% reduction”; “waste sent to the incinerator (white bags)”; “by 2030”
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollutants from incineration; improved resource efficiency/circular economy; potential cost savings in waste treatment; reduced upstream impacts from virgin material production.
instance_edge_case_notes: No instrument is named beyond the numeric reduction goal; classification therefore relies on interpreting it as a target rather than a regulatory mandate or pricing mechanism.
